---
title: "LTH — key results (team summary)"
subtitle: "Heat × wound × *Acropora pulchra*, Mo'orea 2025"
date: "Updated 2026-07-02"
output:
  html_document:
    self_contained: true
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    df_print: kable
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, out.width = "100%", fig.align = "center")
fig  <- function(f) file.path("..", "..", "figures", f)   # code-generated figures
img  <- function(f) file.path("img", f)                    # photos pulled from the deck
```

```{css, echo=FALSE}
.main-container { max-width: 860px; }
/* figure cards */
div.figure { text-align: center; margin: 1.8em auto; }
div.figure img, .content-img {
  max-width: min(100%, 700px);
  border: 1px solid #e4e7eb; border-radius: 6px;
  box-shadow: 0 1px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.09); padding: 6px; background: #fff;
}
p.caption, figcaption { font-size: 0.85em; color: #667; margin-top: 0.6em; }
/* treatment chips */
.t28 { background:#56B4E9; color:#08374f; padding:1px 6px; border-radius:4px; font-weight:600; white-space:nowrap; }
.t31 { background:#D55E00; color:#fff;    padding:1px 6px; border-radius:4px; font-weight:600; white-space:nowrap; }
/* draft banner */
.banner { border-left:6px solid #1F3864; background:#f4f7fb; padding:12px 18px;
  margin:0 0 1.6em 0; border-radius:0 6px 6px 0; font-size:0.95em; color:#33415c; }
/* footer */
.docfooter { margin-top:3em; padding-top:1em; border-top:1px solid #e2e2e2;
  font-size:0.83em; color:#8a94a6; line-height:1.5; }
.docfooter code { font-size:0.95em; }
```

<div class="banner">
<strong>Internal working draft</strong> — a team summary of the LTH phenotype results (RNA-seq still to come). Not for circulation outside the group.
</div>

A quick tour of what the phenotype half of LTH shows. We heated *A. pulchra* fragments (<span class="t28">28 °C</span> vs <span class="t31">31 °C</span>), wounded half of them, and tracked healing, growth, physiology, and symbionts for two weeks. The RNA-seq is still to come; this is the picture the phenotypes paint.

Every number here traces to `output/tables/20_master_results.csv`; the full write-up with limitations is in `RESULTS.md`. Coral photos and the design schematic are from the project deck (M. Brzezinski) — see `img/SLIDE_IMAGES_INDEX.md`.

## The experiment

- 8 tanks — four held at <span class="t28">28 °C</span> (ambient), four at <span class="t31">31 °C</span> (chronic heat, weeks)
- Half the fragments wounded (clipped growing tip), half sham
- 3 field thickets / genets — A, C, D
- Tracked at Days 1, 3, 10, 15

```{r, fig.cap="Experimental design: 8 tanks crossing heat × wound × genet, tracked to Day 15."}
knitr::include_graphics(img("slide05_experimental_design.png"))
```

We wound a fragment by clipping the growing tip; here is a wounded polyp at Day 0.

```{r, fig.cap="A wounded polyp at Day 0 (heated fragment)."}
knitr::include_graphics(img("slide14_morphology_heated_D0.png"))
```

## What we found

### 1. The heat treatment held

Tanks sat cleanly at <span class="t28">28</span> vs <span class="t31">31 °C</span> for the whole run — the +3 °C contrast is real, not noise.

```{r, fig.cap="Per-tank temperature: the two treatments held their target the whole run."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("08_apex_temperature.png"))
```

### 2. Whole-colony physiology drops under heat

Heat, not wounding, drove the colony-wide decline. Under <span class="t31">31 °C</span> corals lost photosynthetic efficiency, paled, grew less, and shed symbionts.

- **Growth: 34% lower** skeletal mass gain at <span class="t31">31 °C</span>
- Symbiont density collapses over the two weeks
- Fv/Fm (photosynthetic efficiency) falls; corals pale on the color card

```{r, fig.cap="Growth (% skeletal mass change): 34% lower under heat."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("05_buoyant_weight_growth.png"))
```

```{r, fig.cap="Symbiont density falls under heat across the two weeks."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("06_symbiont_density_by_day.png"))
```

### 3. Heat blocks regeneration but spares healing — the headline

Wounds close at the same rate whether or not corals are heated. What heat breaks is the **next** step: regrowing skeleton and new corallites.

- New-corallite regeneration stalls under heat (interval-censored time ratio **1.32**, *p* = 1.4e-7; Cox HR **0.22**)
- **67%** of heated corals closed the wound but never regenerated, vs **0%** at ambient
- You can see it in the photos: both temps seal over, but the heated fragments never rebuild the tip

```{r, fig.cap="Time-to-event by treatment: wounds close equally, but new-corallite regeneration stalls under heat."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("14_morphology_KM.png"))
```

The photo timeline makes it plain — both temperatures seal the wound, but the heated fragments never rebuild the tip.

```{r, fig.cap="Photo timeline — ambient (top) vs heated (bottom), Day 0 → Day 15."}
knitr::include_graphics(img("slide15_morphology_timeline.jpg"))
```

Same thing as time-lapse — ambient (left) heals **and** regrows; heated (right) heals but stalls (loops, D0 → D15):

```{r, results='asis'}
vid <- function(f, cap) sprintf(
  '<figure style="display:inline-block;width:49%%;margin:0.3em 0;text-align:center;vertical-align:top">
   <video autoplay loop muted playsinline class="content-img" style="width:100%%">
   <source src="data:video/mp4;base64,%s" type="video/mp4"></video>
   <figcaption>%s</figcaption></figure>',
  xfun::base64_encode(img(f)), cap)
cat(vid("morphology_ambient_healing.mp4", "Ambient — heals and regrows"),
    vid("morphology_heated_healing.mp4", "Heated — heals but stalls"))
```

### 4. Genotype decides how badly

The three genets did not respond alike. Genet **C** held its photochemistry, color, and symbionts far better than A and D.

- Heat hit C about **2.7–3.5× less** than A/D
- Multivariate displacement under heat: C = 1.02 vs A = 3.71, D = 3.38

```{r, fig.cap="Standardized heat sensitivity per genet across responses — C is the resilient outlier."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("19_genet_dashboard.png"))
```

### 5. Chronic, but sublethal

<span class="t31">31 °C</span> sits about **4.4 °C below** the acute Fv/Fm ED50 (35.4 °C; Cunning et al. 2024) for this species at this site. So this is weeks of sub-bleaching stress, not an acute heat shock — the decline is accumulated, not a spike.

```{r, fig.cap="Our 28/31 °C next to the acute CBASS ED50 (Cunning et al. 2024) — chronic but sublethal."}
knitr::include_graphics(fig("26_thermal_context.png"))
```

## Take-homes

- Chronic sub-bleaching heat drives a colony-wide decline — growth down 34%, symbionts and photochemistry down — driven by temperature, not the wound.
- Wounds still **heal** under heat, but corals stop **regenerating** (67% close but never regrow the tip vs 0% at ambient). Healing and regeneration are separate processes, and heat hits only the second.
- Genotype matters: genet C is markedly more resilient, so which colonies you pick changes the outcome.
- At <span class="t31">31 °C</span> this is chronic sub-bleaching stress (~4.4 °C under the acute ED50), not acute shock — a different regime than most heat-stress work.

## Where to find things

- Full results + limitations — `RESULTS.md`
- Every statistic (source of truth) — `output/tables/20_master_results.csv`
- Figure catalog — `figures/FIGURE_INDEX.md`
- Pulled slide photos + provenance — `docs/team_summary/img/SLIDE_IMAGES_INDEX.md`
- RNA-seq handoff (Shreya) — `docs/for_shreya/`

<div class="docfooter">
Repo: <code>stier-lab/17.-LTH_expression_by_temperature_2025</code> · branch <code>docs/annotate-code</code> · source <code>docs/team_summary/LTH_results_summary.Rmd</code><br>
Questions → Adrian Stier / Molly Brzezinski. Analysis figures from the code pipeline; coral photos and design schematic by the LTH field team (M. Brzezinski).
</div>
